notes: Chef review wave 6. Backstage source notes only; public pages should remain recipe-first.

recipes.review_summary: The existing asida was broadly correct. This pass adds date syrup as the more Libyan serving option and keeps the flour-water-butter structure simple.
recommended_changes: Use flour, water, and salt for the mound.
Serve with date syrup, honey, and melted butter.
Keep the method vigorous so the dough turns smooth.
recipes.official_source_status: No single official recipe standard found. Libyan and regional references consistently describe asida as a flour-and-water mound served with date syrup or honey and butter.

recipe.description: Libyan asida, a smooth wheat-flour mound served warm with a well of melted butter, date syrup, or honey.
recipe.story: Asida is humble but not careless. Stir hard until the flour turns elastic, then shape it while warm and let the syrup pool in the center.
recipe.best_served_with: Mint tea, Arabic coffee, date syrup, or honey

recipes.review_summary: Bazin was mostly sound, but the spice profile was too thin for Libyan sauce. The update keeps barley dough, lamb tomato stew, potato, eggs, and adds the expected warm spices.
recommended_changes: Keep barley flour dough and lamb tomato sauce.
Add paprika, cayenne, black pepper, and fenugreek as Libyan sauce notes.
Remove halal as a guaranteed diet tag.
recipes.official_source_status: No single official recipe standard found. Libyan sources agree on barley flour bazin with lamb or mutton tomato sauce, potatoes, and eggs.

sources.title: AP News - Libyan bazin Ramadan dish

recipe.description: Libyan bazin, a dense barley-flour dome surrounded by lamb tomato sauce, potatoes, eggs, and warm spices.
recipe.story: Bazin is communal food. The dough should be firm enough to tear, and the sauce should be rich enough to season every bite.
recipe.best_served_with: Hard-boiled eggs, potatoes, lamb sauce, and simple salad

recipes.review_summary: Sharba Libiya needed missing liquid, oil, chickpeas, lemon, and parsley/cilantro details. The update keeps lamb, tomato paste, orzo, dried mint, and Ramadan-soup identity.
recommended_changes: Add olive oil, water or stock, chickpeas, lemon, parsley, and salt.
Keep dried mint as essential finish.
Use orzo or small bird-tongue pasta.
recipes.official_source_status: No single official recipe standard found. Libyan references consistently describe sharba as lamb or beef soup with tomato paste, orzo, mint, spices, and herbs.

sources.title: Milk Street/AP - Libyan beef and chickpea soup

recipe.description: Libyan lamb and orzo soup with tomato paste, chickpeas, cinnamon, turmeric, dried mint, lemon, and fresh herbs.
recipe.story: Sharba Libiya is the soup people mean when they simply say sharba. It should be meaty, tomato-red, minty, and bright with lemon.
recipe.best_served_with: Bread, dates, lemon wedges, and extra dried mint

replace_steps.title: Brown meat
replace_steps.instruction: Heat olive oil and brown the lamb or beef. Season lightly with salt and pepper.

replace_steps.title: Build base
replace_steps.instruction: Add onion, tomato paste, turmeric, cinnamon, and half the dried mint. Cook until the paste darkens.

replace_steps.title: Simmer soup
replace_steps.instruction: Add chickpeas and water or stock. Simmer until the meat is tender.

replace_steps.title: Add pasta
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in orzo and cook until tender, stirring often so it does not stick.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in lemon juice, parsley or cilantro, and remaining dried mint. Adjust salt and serve hot.

recipes.review_summary: Mofo Gasy was ingredient-correct but the total time ignored fermentation. The update keeps rice flour, yeast, sugar, coconut milk, and mold cooking while making the rise time honest.
recommended_changes: Include one hour rise in prep time.
Keep rice flour and coconut milk as a common coastal/home variation.
Cook in round molds or an aebleskiver pan.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Malagasy sources agree on sweet rice-flour batter cooked in round molds, with regional coconut milk variations.

recipe.description: Malagasy sweet rice-flour breakfast cakes, fermented lightly, cooked in round molds, and served warm with coffee or tea.
recipe.story: Mofo gasy should be softly sweet and lightly yeasted. Give the batter time to wake up before it hits the hot mold.
recipe.best_served_with: Coffee, tea, honey, or fruit jam

recipes.review_summary: Ravitoto needed a longer braise time. Cassava leaves and pork should cook slowly until tender, rich, and earthy.
recommended_changes: Raise cook time for the long cassava-leaf braise.
Keep pork, cassava leaves, garlic, onion, ginger, coconut milk, and rice pairing.
Remove rushed 60-minute total behavior.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Malagasy recipes consistently use pounded cassava leaves with pork, garlic, and a long simmer, often with coconut milk.

recipe.description: Malagasy pounded cassava leaves braised slowly with pork, garlic, ginger, and coconut milk until deep and earthy.
recipe.story: Ravitoto rewards patience. Cook the cassava leaves until their rough edge softens and the pork becomes part of the sauce.

replace_steps.title: Brown pork
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot and brown pork until the fat begins to render.

replace_steps.title: Cook aromatics
replace_steps.instruction: Add onion, garlic, and ginger. Cook until soft, then add tomatoes if using.

replace_steps.title: Add cassava leaves
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in pounded cassava leaves and water. Mix well with the pork and aromatics.

replace_steps.title: Braise slowly
replace_steps.instruction: Cover and simmer on low, stirring occasionally, until the leaves are very tender and the pork is soft.

replace_steps.title: Finish coconut
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in coconut milk and simmer uncovered until the sauce thickens.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Adjust salt and serve hot over white rice with tomato rougail.

recipes.review_summary: Romazava was close but needed anamalaho or paracress named clearly. The update keeps beef, tomato, onion, ginger, mixed greens, and rice, while avoiding extra vegetables that would drift away from the Malagasy profile.
recommended_changes: Name anamalaho or paracress as the distinctive green where available.
Keep rice as the natural pairing.
Remove halal as a guaranteed diet tag.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Malagasy references consistently describe romazava as beef or zebu with greens, especially anamalaho, served with rice.

sources.title: US Department of State IVLP Cookbook - Royal Romazava

recipe.description: Madagascar's beef and greens stew with tomato, onion, ginger, and anamalaho or paracress when available.
recipe.story: Romazava is clean, green, and brothy. Let the beef become tender before the greens go in, then keep the final taste fresh.
recipe.best_served_with: Steamed white rice and tomato rougail

recipes.review_summary: Kondowole was mostly correct. This pass keeps cassava flour and water, adds stronger fish-relish pairing language, and removes halal as unnecessary metadata.
recommended_changes: Keep cassava flour and water only for the staple.
Serve with fish, greens, or tomato-onion relish.
Keep the warning that it becomes very sticky and takes strength to stir.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Malawian references consistently describe kondowole as a northern cassava-flour staple usually eaten with fish.

recipe.description: Northern Malawian cassava-flour staple cooked into a sticky, elastic mound and served with fish or vegetable relish.
recipe.story: Kondowole is simple but physical. Add the cassava flour gradually and stir with confidence until it becomes smooth and stretchy.
recipe.best_served_with: Chambo, dried fish relish, mustard greens, or tomato-onion ndiwo

recipes.review_summary: Mandasi needed honest rise time. The ingredients are plausible for a Malawian/East African fried dough, but the old 40-minute total ignored the one-hour proof.
recommended_changes: Include rise time in prep.
Keep flour, coconut milk or milk, sugar, yeast, cardamom, and frying oil.
Keep vegan only when coconut milk is the default.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. East and Southern African mandazi/mandasi sources agree on flour, sugar, yeast or baking powder, milk or coconut milk, spice, and frying.

recipe.description: Malawian mandasi, lightly sweet fried dough made with coconut milk, yeast, cardamom, and a proper rise before frying.
recipe.story: Mandasi should not be rushed. Let the dough puff, then fry gently so the inside cooks before the outside gets too dark.
recipe.best_served_with: Tea, coffee, fruit jam, or powdered sugar

recipes.review_summary: Nsima with Chambo was close, but it should name chambo as the preferred fish with tilapia as a substitute and should not advertise halal automatically.
recommended_changes: Use chambo where available, tilapia as substitute.
Keep nsima, lemon, garlic, tomato-onion ndiwo, and grilling method.
Remove halal metadata.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Malawi references consistently describe nsima as the staple and chambo as a prized Lake Malawi fish served grilled or fried.

sources.title: Tellerrand Stories - Malawi's main dish Nsima

recipe.description: Malawian nsima served with grilled chambo or tilapia, tomato-onion ndiwo, lemon, and a firm maize-meal mound.
recipe.story: The plate is simple and proud: firm nsima, fresh lake fish, and a relish bright enough to pull everything together.
recipe.best_served_with: Tomato-onion ndiwo, mustard greens, lemon wedges, or Nali-style hot sauce

recipes.review_summary: Fakoye needed its leaf-sauce identity corrected. Okra can support texture, but the defining ingredient is fakoye or jute leaf, often with meat or dried fish and soumbala.
recommended_changes: Use fakoye, jute, or molokhia leaves as the base.
Keep soumbala/dawadawa and dried fish as common flavor builders.
Make okra optional instead of defining the dish.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Mali and Sahel references describe fakoye/fakou as a northern Malian leaf sauce from jute or similar leaves, often with meat or fish.

sources.title: Comboni Missionaries - Rice in Fakoye Sauce

recipe.description: Northern Malian fakoye leaf sauce with jute or molokhia leaves, dried fish, soumbala, onion, chile, and optional okra.
recipe.story: Fakoye is a leaf sauce first. The pot should turn silky and deep, with soumbala and dried fish giving it its old-school backbone.
recipe.best_served_with: Toh, rice, millet paste, or steamed fonio

replace_steps.title: Start base
replace_steps.instruction: Heat palm oil or shea butter and cook onion until soft. Stir in soumbala.

replace_steps.title: Add liquid and protein
replace_steps.instruction: Add water or stock, dried fish, and hot pepper. Simmer to season the broth.

replace_steps.title: Add leaves
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in fakoye leaves or powder. Cook until the sauce darkens and becomes silky.

replace_steps.title: Finish texture
replace_steps.instruction: Add okra if using and simmer until the sauce is lightly thickened. Adjust salt.

recipes.review_summary: Tigadegena needed garlic, liquid, and vegetable range. The corrected version keeps peanut paste, tomato, meat, and optional vegetables while making the sauce easier to cook without scorching.
recommended_changes: Add garlic, stock or water, and optional cabbage or sweet potato.
Loosen peanut paste before adding.
Remove halal as guaranteed metadata.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Malian and West African sources agree on peanut paste, tomato, onion, meat or chicken, and vegetables.

sources.title: The Food Dictator - Malian Peanut Stew

recipe.description: Malian peanut stew with lamb, beef, or chicken simmered in tomato, onion, garlic, groundnut paste, and vegetables.
recipe.story: Tigadegena is all about a smooth peanut sauce. Loosen the paste first, simmer low, and stir so the groundnuts do not catch.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, toh, fonio, couscous, or steamed millet

replace_ingredients.name: natural peanut paste or peanut butter

recipes.review_summary: Toh was already in good shape. This pass keeps millet or sorghum flour, water, salt, and steady stirring as the full recipe.
recommended_changes: Keep millet or sorghum flour.
Use slurry first to reduce lumps.
Serve with tigadegena, fakoye, okra, or leafy sauce.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Malian references consistently describe tô/toh as a millet, sorghum, or maize paste eaten with sauce.

recipe.description: Malian toh, a firm millet or sorghum paste stirred smooth and shaped into a mound for sauces.
recipe.story: Toh looks simple, but the spoon work matters. Build it with a slurry, then stir until glossy and firm.
recipe.best_served_with: Tigadegena, fakoye, okra sauce, leaf sauce, or meat stew
